FOR DESSERT: UP ON THE HOUSETOP, CRAB LEG CLICKS

WKRC: “Authorities confirmed that a drone was intercepted at a prison. According to a statement posted to X on Monday by the S.C. Department of Corrections, a drone arrived at Lee Correctional Institution carrying multiple items, including crab legs, seasoning and marijuana. ‘Seems some folks were planning an early holiday Old Bay crab boil and steak dinner along with their marijuana and cigarettes — all dropped by a drone at Lee CI,’  the post reads. The image appears to show Old Bay seasoning, Marlboro cigarettes, crab legs, and steak. Authorities did not confirm whether any arrests were made in connection with the incident.”

data.title: Marijuana use under the microscope: Could your state revoke legal weed?
data.description:
Here’s some good news: Residents of Massachusetts want to roll back recreational marijuana use in their state.

Just when you thought that none of the Democrat-embraced initiatives damaging our country — eliminating cash bail, dumbing down our public schools or encouraging widespread pot use — would ever be overturned, here comes an effort in the Bay State to put legal weed use to a vote.

This makes Massachusetts the first state in the country to challenge marijuana legalization; with any luck, it won’t be the last. 

Many Americans appear to be souring on legal pot, so the effort in Massachusetts comes at a good time. Numerous studies have concluded that regular use of cannabis brings serious health risks, as well as brain impairment among young people. Promoters of the industry are working overtime to convince voters that marijuana is a harmless product; there is mounting evidence that today’s pot, which is many times more potent than the product enjoyed by Boomers in the 1960s and 1970s, is dangerous to individuals and to society. 

Gallup shows Americans’ enthusiasm for legal pot at a six-year low of 64 percent, down from an all-time high of 70 percent in 2023.  

Democrats in charge of states like Colorado, Washington and Hawaii rushed over a decade ago to legalize recreational cannabis use. Why? Because blue-state officials saw selling and taxing weed as a great opportunity to rake in cash and help offset their out-of-control spending.  

In 2012, Colorado became the first state to legalize recreational use of weed, along with Washington state, after approving medical use in 2000. In the years leading up to the referendum on legal pot, Colorado faced major budget deficits; in 2011, the Centennial State faced a gap of $1.5 billion. Advocates promised voters that the state’s fiscal problems would be solved by the taxes generated by legal weed sales.     

Lost in the arguments over prospective tax revenues — which failed to materialize at expected levels, by the way — was sufficient consideration of the long-term effects of marijuana use. Even as the legalization push in Colorado raged on, studies emerged showing that frequent cannabis use, especially among young people, could cause memory loss, learning impairment and a high potential for addiction. In high doses, the drug could lead to psychosis and paranoia.  

After several years of expanded marijuana legalization and use, the reports have only become more alarming. The American College of Cardiologists published a report this year saying that a study of 4.6 million people “showed a 50 percent increased risk [of heart attack] among those who used the drug.” It also indicated that “cannabis users younger than age 50 were over six times as likely to suffer a heart attack compared to non-users.”

Surveys have also shown that prenatal exposure can have dire consequences for newborns — frequent use raises the risk of stroke and smoking pot also carries the potential for lung disease. Studies have also linked pot use with increasing numbers of arrests and convictions for driving under the influence. 

Not surprisingly, as states legalized weed, usage has grown. After all, the message from local officials is that using pot is okay. A National Survey on Drug Use and Health between 2002 and 2019 reported that the proportion of the U.S. population over 12 years of age who consumed marijuana in the prior year increased from 11 percent in 2002 to 18 percent in 2019. Young people especially have increased their use of cannabis, with about 36 percent of 12th graders and 43 percent of college students reporting use in 2021.  

Faced with the growing mountain of evidence that using cannabis is bad for young people and bad for our country, the Coalition for a Healthy Massachusetts has rounded up more than the 74,000 signatures required to put restricting legal pot on the 2026 ballot. Although the group must jump through further legal hurdles, voters will almost certainly get a chance to reject the free-flowing sale and recreational use of marijuana in their state.

“An Act to Restore a Sensible Marijuana Policy” would reverse parts of the legalization measure passed by 54 percent of Massachusetts voters in 2016. It would revise both the tax and regulatory framework of the state’s cannabis laws; it would not affect the sale and use of medical marijuana. The committee’s proposal would ban people from growing marijuana plants in their homes and also prohibit the retail sale of cannabis products. 

Opponents of the measure point, predictably, to the loss of tax revenues if the ban were to pass. Since licensed stores opened their doors in December 2018, Massachusetts customers have bought nearly $9 billion of cannabis and related products, delivering almost $1.5 billion in tax revenue to the state. Pot proponents claim that some 27,000 people are employed by the industry.   

Wendy Wakeman is chairman and spokesperson for the coalition seeking to overturn Massachusetts’ laws. In a phone interview, she said her group was alarmed that the rapid roll-out of pot legalization meant the substance was virtually unregulated. Also offensive is that usage in public areas like the Boston Commons is pervasive, even though drinking alcohol in such places is illegal. She and what she describes as a bipartisan group of parents, doctors and educators feel that the negative impact of widespread use — the health and safety issues — is “understudied.”

Understudied perhaps, but evidence of real risks continues to pile up. A recent review by Mass General Brigham Hospital found that, after legalization, the rate of cannabis use by adolescents jumped more than three-fold, as did the number of young people admitted for psychiatric emergencies.

Under federal law, cannabis sales and consumption are still illegal. Pot companies are pleading with President Trump to change that. There is big money at stake; this year, industry sales are expected to near $50 billion.

But there are also big risks. Hopefully, this Massachusetts effort is just the beginning of a nationwide conversation about the dangers of legal weed. And hopefully, Trump hears these concerns.  

Liz Peek is a former partner of major bracket Wall Street firm Wertheim and Company.

data.title: Trump says he’s just pardoning Tina Peters convicted in state court
data.description:
President Trump on Thursday pardoned former Colorado clerk Tina Peters, who was convicted of election interference in state court in August 2024.

“Tina is sitting in a Colorado prison for the ‘crime’ of demanding Honest Elections,” Trump wrote in a post on Truth Social. “Today I am granting Tina a full Pardon for her attempts to expose Voter Fraud in the Rigged 2020 Presidential Election!”

The decision comes after Trump pressured Colorado Gov. Jared Polis (D) for months to free Peters, threatening “harsh measures” if not.

The former clerk was found guilty on seven counts, including four felonies for election interference. She was sentenced to nine years in prison, where her lawyers say she was the subject of numerous attacks.

“This lightweight Governor, who has allowed his State to go to hell (Tren de Aragua, anyone?), should be ashamed of himself,” Trump wrote online in early December.

“FREE TINA!”

The Trump administration and Federal Bureau of Prisons sent a letter to the Colorado Department of Corrections last month seeking to transfer Peters from state to federal custody. The request was ultimately denied.

Earlier this month, the president urged state leaders to release Peters, noting that she is a cancer survivor.

Prosecutors allege the ex-election official stole a county employee’s security badge to help a man access the voting systems in May 2021 to assist MyPillow CEO Mike Lindell’s false claims about the 2020 presidential election. 

Colorado Attorney General Phil Weiser (D) says the move “undermines” the state judicial system.

“Our system of government gives states authority to run their own criminal justice systems. As you noted, there was a trial, there was a conviction by a jury, there’s an appeal to the state courts,” he said during an appearance on the local NBC affiliate station 9News’s “Next with Kyle Clark.”

“All that is happening under the rule of law,” he added. “This president doesn’t respect the rule of law, but he doesn’t have authority to undermine how we operate our judicial system here in Colorado.”

data.title: ‘America First’ starts in our own hemisphere
data.description:
There’s a reason American airmen, sailors, and Marines are putting themselves into harm’s way in the southern Caribbean. It’s not because Washington suddenly returned to neoconservative adventurism. It is because a narco-state ruled by a corrupt leftist dictator has become the latest frontline in defending the American people.

That might surprise some who still believe President Trump’s “America First” ideal means “America only.” But what’s happening off the coast of Venezuela tells a different story. The fight against Nicolás Maduro’s criminal regime isn’t about foreign crusades, but about keeping poison off our streets and chaos off our shores.

For a generation, U.S. national security policy wandered the earth, guarding oil fields in Syria while leaving American towns in Texas undefended. We policed deserts halfway around the world but let narco-fleets cruise the Caribbean, ferrying the chemicals that kill tens of thousands of Americans every year. Something had gone badly wrong.

Trump came back to fix it. Among the hundreds of executive orders he signed in his first days back in office was one that should have been obvious but somehow wasn’t: The mission of the U.S. Armed Forces is to defend U.S. territory and the American people — period.

That clarity is now visible on the high seas. American forces are sinking narco-trafficking vessels before they can deliver their deadly cargo to our shores. Every shipment stopped is a community defended. In the Ohio River Valley, in the rural Southeast, across the great West. This isn’t foreign intervention — it is self-defense with a longer reach.

Predictably, the usual suspects in Washington clutch their pearls. “This isn’t our problem to fix,” they say.

They forget (or choose to ignore) that we are no longer fighting against mere criminals. These aren’t just drug traffickers anymore — they are militarized networks with a political agenda. They control territory, traffic people, bribe governments, and operate freely with the backing of states that despise us.

In Venezuela, the Cartel de los Soles and the Tren de Aragua gang don’t just work with the Maduro regime — they are the regime. From Mexico to Cuba to Nicaragua, the same pattern holds: Cartels have captured or partnered with the highest elements of the government. That deadly symbiosis makes this a state threat, and state threats demand state responses.

Trump understands that national security begins at home — and “home” includes the entire Western Hemisphere. America’s security depends directly on the stability of our neighbors. When Latin America sinks into crime and corruption, it doesn’t stay there. It lands at our border, in our towns, and in our morgues.

The fentanyl killing our children, the migration waves overwhelming our systems, the foreign influence creeping closer to our shores — none of this is happening in isolation. It all connects back to the same source: weak, lawless, or hostile regimes in our own neighborhood.

That’s why the strikes against cartel infrastructure in Venezuela and the broader military buildup in the Caribbean aren’t just tactics. They’re messages. They declare the era of impunity for the cartels is over. For years, these organizations waged war on the U.S. and were treated like common criminals. Those days have ended. The U.S. will act first to protect its citizens, not wait for the traffickers to show up at our borders.

And that message isn’t only for Caracas. It is for every capital that believes that partnering with cartels or with our foes comes free of consequences. The hemisphere’s security is our first line of defense. Remove the hub of instability in Venezuela, and the entire region becomes safer, freer, and less hospitable to America’s enemies.

None of this is new. It’s the logic of the Monroe Doctrine and the Roosevelt Corollary that followed it. When chaos or lawlessness takes hold in the hemisphere and local governments can’t or won’t act, the U.S. must. Because if we don’t, that disorder and criminality spreads and hits American communities and homes.

It is a fantasy to pretend that our domestic crises are unrelated to the collapse of order in our neighborhood. Every overdose, every surge at the border, every cartel war that spills northward is a symptom of the same disease: neglect of our own hemisphere. And the rest of the world will not just politely wait while America sorts itself out. When we turn inward, the world won’t pause. The vacuum fills quickly, and not with friends.

The U.S. can’t afford to play defense only at its borders.

Trump’s test for every policy is simple: Does it make America safer, stronger, and more prosperous? If the answer is yes, then it’s worth doing. Removing a narco-regime tied to China, Iran, and Russia passes that test with flying colors. It’s not a distraction from America First, it’s the clearest expression of it.

Securing the homeland begins with securing the neighborhood. A strong America leads close to home first. Because if we don’t, others will, and they won’t wait their turn. No cause is more righteous than that, or more American.

Melissa Ford serves as the director of the Western Hemisphere Initiative at the America First Policy Institute.

data.title: Trump eyes road show after GOP losses
data.description:
Morning Report is The Hill’s a.m. newsletter. Subscribe here.

In today’s issue:

▪ Trump poised to hit the road in new year

▪ Indiana Republicans reject redistricting

▪ White House doubles down on Venezuela

▪ Supreme Court considers taking up gun cases

President Trump relayed to a crowd of supporters in Pennsylvania earlier this week that chief of staff Susie Wiles told him to “start campaigning,” an apparent nudge following a stretch of defeats for the Republican Party and backlash over his handling of Americans’ affordability concerns.

Those comments appear to be setting the stage for Trump to hit the road more often in 2026 and defend his record after a relatively light interstate travel schedule during his first year back in the Oval Office.

The Hill’s Julia Manchester reports that the GOP is spooked by recent special election losses and underperformances in Republican strongholds, as the party heads into next year’s midterm elections with pressure to hold onto its slim majorities in both the House and Senate.

The president’s Pennsylvania pitstop marked what the White House said would be the first in a series of trips focusing on the issue of affordability, Manchester reports. Wiles herself said Trump will ramp up his travel ahead of the midterms, telling the “Mom View” podcast that the president is “going to campaign like it’s 2024 again.”

Republicans have faced jitters after a string of party losses in local and statewide elections across the country this fall.

Trump drew a more direct rebuke Thursday when the Republican-led Indiana Legislature rejected a new GOP-favored U.S. House map, despite intense lobbying by the president, The Hill’s Caroline Vakil writes.

The state Senate voted 31-19 against the map, which would have likely netted the GOP two more U.S. House seats. For weeks legislators had voiced skepticism about the redistricting effort, but the vote was notable as it marked a rare moment of GOP defiance against the president’s wishes.

Trump vowed to primary Indiana Republicans who didn’t vote for the map, escalating already simmering party tensions.

“If Republicans will not do what is necessary to save our Country, they will eventually lose everything to the Democrats,” Trump warned Wednesday on Truth Social.

▪ The Hill: Indiana Senate rejects new House map, defying Trump

▪ Indiana Capital Chronicle: Senate Republicans reject Trump’s plea for gerrymandered maps

Thursday’s setback for Trump followed a set of GOP electoral headaches after losing various races across the country in recent weeks.

Eileen Higgins won Miami’s mayoral election on Tuesday, becoming the first Democrat elected mayor of the South Florida city in three decades. Miami narrowly went for former Vice President Kamala Harris last year, but Trump flipped Miami-Dade County, which includes the city.

Democrats also flipped a conservative-leaning state House district in Georgia that the president carried by 12 points last year.

And while a Republican candidate won last week’s special election in Tennessee, Rep. Matt Van Epps (R) triumphed by roughly 9 points, a far smaller margin than the 22 points Trump carried the district by in 2024.

Democrats also won all key races in November’s off-year elections.

“There’s a lot of digging our heads in the sand and acting as if we don’t have a five-alarm fire going off,” a source close to the White House told Manchester.

The defeats for Trump and the GOP have run parallel to the criticisms the president has endured around affordability, which he called a “Democrat scam” but more recently has attempted to change the narrative about.

An AP-NORC survey released Thursday found just 31 percent of respondents favor the president’s handling of the economy — a new low.

On Tuesday in Mount Pocono, Pa., the president acknowledged prices need to come down even as he touted the economy in his second term.

“I can’t say affordability hoax because I agree the prices were too high, so I can’t call it a hoax because they’ll misconstrue that,’” Trump said.

▪ NPR: Trump calls affordability crisis a ‘hoax.’ The data tells a different story

▪ The Hill: GOP lawmakers unhappy with Trump’s affordability message: ‘You can’t call it a hoax’

Trump is not the only one with a beefed-up travel schedule.

Vice President Vance is scheduled to visit the Keystone State next week, with planned remarks in Allentown on Tuesday where he will reportedly echo Trump’s message on the economy.

If the president does attend more rallies in 2026 as the White House has promised, it will mark a change from this year, when his trips have mostly focused on international travel.

The relative lack of U.S. trips pale in comparison to the aftermath of the 2016 election, when Trump held a Florida campaign rally in February 2017 — less than a month into his first term.

Republicans are hoping that a more visible Trump next year will provide a boost for GOP candidates heading into the crucial midterms.

“I’m all for it, I think we should try it out,” Mike Dubke, who served as White House communications director during Trump’s first administration, told Manchester.

3 Things to Know Today

1. Trump on Thursday night signed an executive order to impose a national standard on artificial intelligence, seeking to limit states’ efforts to enact their own AI laws.

2. The Department of Justice again failed to secure a new indictment against New York Attorney General Letitia James (D) after its initial case against her was dismissed, marking the second time a grand jury has refused to approve charges against her this month.

3. A federal judge ordered immigration officials to immediately releaseKilmar Abrego Garcia, who was wrongfully deported to El Salvador earlier this year. He still faces criminal charges in the U.S.

Leading the Day

NOW WHAT?: The Senate on Thursday failed to advance dueling bills tied to the soon-to-expire Affordable Care Act (ACA) enhanced subsidies, teeing up a January health care battle ahead of another potential government shutdown.

The GOP-led proposal would have converted funding for the ACA tax credits into contributions toward health savings accounts to pay for out-of-pocket costs.

The other bill that failed, championed by Senate Minority Leader Chuck Schumer (D-N.Y.), proposed a three-year extension of the subsidies.

While the competing legislation went down largely along party lines, four GOP lawmakers backed the Democratic proposal: Sens. Susan Collins (Maine), Lisa Murkowski (Alaska), Dan Sullivan (Alaska) and Josh Hawley (Mo.). Collins and Sullivan are both up for reelection next year.

“I’m really an all-of-the-above-approach kind of guy. At this point, I think we need to do everything we can to bring down the cost of premiums,” Hawley told reporters Thursday.

▪ The Hill: More on the four GOP senators who broke ranks.

▪ The New York Times: Senate Deadlocks on Health Care, Leaving Subsidies to Expire.

The attention will now turn to the House, where Republican leaders are preparing a vote on another partisan health care package that does not include the ACA subsidies.

But The Hill’s Nathaniel Weixel and Mike Lillis report that rank-and-file moderates in both parties are agitating for compromise, launching a pair of discharge petitions this week designed to force votes over the opposition of Speaker Mike Johnson (R-La.) and other GOP leaders.

Still, lawmakers in both parties say there’s almost no chance that a breakthrough deal will emerge before Congress leaves Washington for the long December break. 

“I would not bet on that at all. I think it’s destined for January,” Rep. Jeff Van Drew (R-N.J.) told The Hill.

REACTIONS TO INDIANA: Trump on Thursday lashed out at Indiana state Senate Republican Leader Rodric Bray, predicting he will lose his next primary following the Legislature’s failed redistricting vote.

“I heard he was against it. He’ll probably lose his next primary whenever that is. I hope he does because he’s done a tremendous disservice,” Trump told reporters in the Oval Office.

The Indiana effort was important for the president, who for months has pushed Republican-led states to redraw their district maps to give the GOP a boost heading into the midterms.

Trump adviser Alex Bruesewitz echoed the president’s frustrations on Thursday night.

“The spineless RINOs in Indiana, many hailing from districts where President Trump won by over 20% just last November have stabbed their own voters in the back and sold out America!” Bruesewitzwrote on the social platform X.

Speaker Johnson said that while the result was a “disappointing outcome,” he is “bullish” on next year’s elections.

“There’s about 14 states at some stage of the process now, either litigation or redistricting. So, we shall see,” he said. “I know that we are going to win because we have a great record to run on.”

▪ Fox News: Trump dealt major blow as Indiana Senate rejects new map.

▪ The New York Times: Trump Offers Symbolic Pardon for Clerk Convicted of Election Tampering.

OIL TANKER FALLOUT: A day after the U.S. seized a Venezuelan oil tanker off the country’s coast, Democrats are sounding alarm bells and saying the move could lead to increased conflict or even war in the region.

The seizure marked the latest move in Trump’s campaign against Venezuelan President Nicolás Maduro, whom the administration has accused of leading a drug cartel in the South American country.

Maduro has denied the allegations, but the U.S. has nonetheless amassed a significant military presence in the region in recent months. Trump has also hinted at ordering strikes on drug traffickers in Venezuela, saying in the Oval Office on Thursday night that such attacks could start on land “pretty soon.”

Sen. Rand Paul (R-Ky.) and some Democratic lawmakers expressed concern about the tanker seizure not long after it was revealed on Wednesday. Russia, meanwhile, has demanded that the administration explain why what Trump described as a “very large” tanker was taken.

“It is needlessly and recklessly escalating a potential conflict,” said Sen. Richard Blumenthal (D-Conn.), a member of the Senate Armed Services Committee. “Trump seems to be stumbling into war without any endgame or strategy.”

▪ The Hill: US seizes oil tanker near Venezuela: What to know

▪ Reuters: US issues fresh sanctions targeting Maduro’s family, Venezuela-linked oil tankers

White House press secretary Karoline Leavitt downplayed the concerns on Thursday, saying the president does not view the seizure as an escalation toward war. She added that Trump is not interested in “prolonged war” with Venezuela and considers Wednesday’s action as simply “effectuating the administration’s sanctions policies.”

“He wants peace. He also wants to see the end of illegal drugs being trafficked into the United States and taking the lives of hundreds of thousands of Americans across the country,” Leavitt told reporters.

AU REVOIR: Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem was accused by Democrats of misleading House Homeland Security Committee members on Thursday when she left a congressional hearing early to attend another meeting that was canceled.

The other meeting, a gathering of the Federal Emergency Management Agency (FEMA) Council, was canceled because White House officials had not been fully briefed on the latest draft of a report detailing plans to reform the agency, an administration official told The Hill’s Rachel Frazin.

The official said the FEMA meeting was canceled Thursday morning while Noem was testifying before Congress and it is likely she didn’t know of the cancellation at the time.

Noem’s office also told The Hill’s Rebecca Beitsch that the secretary only found out about the FEMA meeting after she left the witness table.

▪ Politico: Noem defiant as Democrats decry Trump administration treatment of migrants.

▪ The Washington Post: White House officials cancel meeting set to help chart FEMA’s future.

Rep. Bennie Thompson (D-Miss.) moved to subpoena Noem to reappear before the panel, saying she shirked her duties and may have even lied about why she departed early.

The tensions come amid reported issues between Noem and the White House, though Trump has denied such reports, saying Wednesday he is “so happy with her.”

Where & When

The president will swear in the chair of the U.S. Export-Import Bank and the CEO of the U.S. International Development Finance Corporation on Friday. He is expected to attend the Army-Navy college football game on Saturday in Baltimore.

The Senate is out today.

The House Energy and Commerce Committee will meet at 9 a.m. for member day.

Zoom In

SECOND AMENDMENT CHALLENGES: An unusually large number of Second Amendment challenges are stacking up at the Supreme Court, and the justices will decide behind closed doors today which ones, if any, they will consider, The Hill’s Zach Schonfeld reports.

The pileup comes during an already big year for gun rights cases, as justices already decided to hear two cases early next year: One on whether unlawful drug users can carry firearms and one on Hawaii’s gun permitting regime.

Most of the new challenges are concerned with regulations barring convicted felons from possessing firearms. More than 10 percent of prosecuted federal charges involve felon-in-possession convictions, according to the U.S. Sentencing Commission.

But the provision has come under increased scrutiny in the wake of the Supreme Court’s gun rights expansion, Schonfeld writes. Three years ago, the conservative majority announced a new test that requires gun control measures to be consistent with the nation’s historical tradition of firearm regulation to comply with the Second Amendment. Since that ruling, criminal defendants across the country have been asking justices to hear their constitutional defenses. 

The justices have rejected many attempts, but that could change at their weekly conference today, during which they will hear more than 60 defendants’ petitions challenging the law, according to a review of the docket by The Hill. 

In addition to the petitions regarding felons in possession of firearms, the court will decide whether to hear a case from a lawsuit filed against Cook County, Ill., by people and gun rights groups who challenge the county’s AR-15 ban, Schonfeld reports.

The court declined in June to hear a case regarding Maryland’s AR-15 ban, with three justices — one short of the four required to take up the case — voting yes. Justice Brett Kavanaugh wanted to wait until the issue had percolated in other appeals courts.

“This case provides the Court with a vehicle for following through on Justice Kavanaugh’s recommendation,” the challengers wrote. 

Like the felon-in-possession cases, the court relisted the AR-15 ban petition, meaning the justices considered them at last week’s conference but took no action, The Hill’s Schonfeld writes.

Two additional cases the justices will consider are challenging high-capacity magazine bans. State and local governments have taken steps to ban the magazines, though the Supreme Court has not settled the issue.

Challengers to bans in California and Washington state hope their cases will change that, and the justices have relisted both petitions for today’s conference. 

▪  SCOTUSblog: Relist rodeo: firearm restrictions, searches incident to arrest, DNA evidence, and ‘clearly established’ law

Elsewhere

CALIFORNIA COLLISION COURSE: California Gov. Gavin Newsom (D) and former Vice President Kamala Harris have enjoyed a kind of distanced relationship all the way back to when they were sworn in to local political offices — Harris as a city district attorney and Newsom as mayor — in San Francisco more than 20 years ago.

Both Californians have had similar voters, fundraisers, consultants and advisers but have strategically, and methodically, stayed out of each other’s way.

But now, with 2028 on the horizon, they are set on a collision course, The Hill’s Amie Parnes reports.

Harris, coming off a bruising and truncated 2024 campaign, is not giving up on politics. Though she’s said that a 2028 campaign is not her priority, it has not been ruled out. And, with the influence — and donors — she still enjoys, it’s hard to believe that she won’t be a contender.

Newsom is already seen by Democrats as the party’s early front-runner, though he has not yet officially launched a campaign — most likely choosing to wait until his time as governor is up in 2027.

But both politicians are eyeing one another.

“You know that expression, ‘Every time a friend succeeds, I die a little?’ I think there’s a little bit of truth when it comes to them,” Elizabeth Ashford, a Democratic consultant who served as Harris’s chief of staff when she was California’s attorney general, told Parnes. Ashford added that the two Californians have always been “competitive but respectful.”

Newsom’s allies think Harris won’t run, Parnes reports, and point to her book “107 Days,” a title reflecting the short period of time she had to run her 2024 campaign. In the book, she took shots at former President Biden, Pennsylvania Gov. Josh Shapiro (D) and other political figures.

But, while Harris isn’t yet committing to another run for president, she’s laying the same kind of groundwork one would do if planning to run. A political action committee, Fight for the People, was set up by her aides.

She’s also been making the rounds on the phone, calling New York City Mayor-elect Zohran Mamdani (D) the day after the democratic socialist won the race for mayor. She offered congratulations to Abigail Spanberger (D) and Mikie Sherrill (D), who respectively won the Virginia and New Jersey gubernatorial races, along with making 40 other calls to other newly elected officials, The New York Times reported.

If they do end up running, the two California “frenemies” will force those close to them to choose a side. Donors, who are close to both, say they are also conflicted.

“I think many of us would support both in the beginning,” said one donor in the state who isn’t convinced Newsom and Harris will both run for the White House. “It’s being asked to choose between mom and dad.”

▪ New York Post: Kamala Harris flatters ‘very talented’ Gavin Newsom amid texting spat

Opinion

Robby Soave in The Hill: Rand Paul demands vote on war in Venezuela. Is there only one principled person left in the Senate?

Don Wolfensberger in The Hill: Should due process be observed in House ethics cases?

The Closer

And finally … 👏👏👏 Congrats to this week’s Morning Report quiz winners! They certainly know their stuff on the ACA!

🧩Here’s who went 4/4: Richard Baznik, Stan Wasser, Rick Schmidtke, Jess Elger, Laura Rettaliata, Lori Benso, Michael Kitz, Pam Manges, Charles Cogar, Dennis Barksdale, Mark Williamson, Carmine Petracca, Robert Bradley and Linda Field.

Former President Obama signed the Affordable Care Act into law in 2010, but main provisions of the law didn’t take effect until 2014.

Massachusetts Sen. Ted Kennedy‘s (D) death in 2009 ultimately proved critical in scaling back the scope of the ACA. Republican Scott Brown pulled off an upset to win a special election to succeed Kennedy in 2010, breaking the Democrats’ filibuster-proof majority with 41 Republican senators. That forced Obama and Democratic congressional leaders to make more concessions to get the law across the finish line.

GOP Sens. John McCain (Ariz.), Susan Collins (Maine) and Lisa Murkowski (Alaska) all voted against the repeal attempt, but Sen. Pat Toomey (R-Pa.) voted for it.

The latest Gallup poll showed 57 percent of Americans said they approve of the ACA, the highest percentage the pollster has recorded.

data.title: Supreme Court confronts gun rights pileup
data.description:
An unusually large pileup of Second Amendment challenges has landed at the Supreme Court, which will consider taking up the cases behind closed doors Friday. 

It would add to what is already a major term for gun rights. The justices previously agreed to hear two cases early next year that involve whether unlawful drug users can carry firearms and Hawaii’s gun permitting regime. 

As those cases move ahead, the new petitions present additional opportunities for the conservative majority to make clear the bounds of their recent gun rights expansion.

Felon in possession

Most of the new challenges concern the federal law that makes it a crime for felons to possess a firearm.  

It’s one of the most prosecuted federal charges. Of the 61,678 cases reported to the U.S. Sentencing Commission in fiscal year 2024, roughly 6,700 of them — more than 10 percent — involved felon-in-possession convictions. 

The man who attempted to assassinate President Trump at his Florida golf course was convicted of the charge in September. 

But the provision has come under increased scrutiny in the wake of the Supreme Court’s gun rights expansion. Three years ago, the conservative majority announced a new test that requires gun control measures to be consistent with the nation’s historical tradition of firearm regulation to comply with the Second Amendment. 

Ever since that ruling, criminal defendants across the country have pleaded with the justices to hear their constitutional defenses.  

The justices have rejected dozens of attempts, but that could soon change. 

At the Supreme Court’s weekly conference on Friday, it will consider more than 60 defendants’ petitions challenging the law, according to The Hill’s review of the court’s docket.

The nine justices meet behind closed doors with no clerks or aides allowed inside the room, and the court typically announces any cases they’ve agreed to take up either later Friday or Monday. 

Notably, many of the petitions have been “relisted,” meaning the justices considered them at last week’s conference but took no action. Instead, they punted and put the petitions on the list again for this week’s meeting. 

It’s a sign that at least one justice is taking a close look. When the court wants to take up a case, it nearly always relists it first. Other times, the court relists a petition when it plans to turn away the case but a justice is drafting a written dissent or statement. 

It remains unclear why the justices have relisted the dozens of felon-in-possession challenges. If they punt again at this week’s meeting, the justices’ next conference won’t take place until the new year. 

The consideration comes as the justices have already agreed to hear a challenge to a neighboring provision of federal law that makes it a crime for unlawful drug users to possess firearms.  

The Trump administration acknowledged “some disagreement” in the lower courts about the felon-in-possession law but urged the Supreme Court to turn away the challenges.  

It pointed to the administration’s revitalization of a program that enables felons to appeal to Attorney General Pam Bondi to get their gun rights back by showing they aren’t a danger to public safety. 

“Any disagreement among the circuits likely lacks prospective importance—and may even evaporate entirely—because of the recent revitalization,” the Justice Department wrote in court filings. 

AR-15 bans

In June, the Supreme Court declined to hear a challenge to Maryland’s AR-15 ban.  

Only three conservatives voted to take the case: Justices Clarence Thomas, Samuel Alito and Neil Gorsuch. That’s one short of the four required.  

But Justice Brett Kavanaugh wrote a three-page statement suggesting that he’d provide his vote once the issue percolates more in other appeals courts, calling AR-15 bans “questionable.” 

“In my view, this Court should and presumably will address the AR–15 issue soon, in the next Term or two,” Kavanaugh wrote. 

Now in the next term, the issue has returned to the Supreme Court. This time, the lawsuit at hand comes from individuals and gun rights groups, who challenge an AR-15 ban in Cook County, Ill., which includes Chicago. 

“This case provides the Court with a vehicle for following through on Justice Kavanaugh’s recommendation,” the challengers wrote. 

They are represented by Cooper & Kirk, a Washington, D.C.-based law firm that regularly represents parties in gun rights cases. 

Cook County is urging the Supreme Court to stay out.  

The county argues the challengers rushed to court to lose and seek the justices’ review, saying they forfeited their Second Amendment arguments by failing to compile an “adequate” factual record for the lower judges. 

“In fact, this case only demonstrates that significant percolation among the lower courts will be necessary before the constitutionality of assault weapons regulations – indeed, the constitutionality of any regulation of a particular kind of weapon – is ripe for this Court’s final adjudication,” the county wrote. 

Like the felon-in-possession cases, the court also relisted the AR-15 ban petition.

Ten states have passed assault weapons bans, though the definition varies in each law, according to Everytown for Gun Safety, a group that supports stronger gun control measures.

High-capacity magazine bans

State and local bans on high-capacity magazines have also become a front in the battles over the Second Amendment, but the Supreme Court has yet to definitively settle the issue. 

Challengers to bans imposed in California and Washington state hope their cases will change things, and the justices have relisted both petitions for this week’s conference. 

Both groups of challengers are represented by Clement & Murphy, the law firm founded by prominent conservative lawyers Paul Clement and Erin Murphy. Clement argued the three biggest gun rights cases decided by the Supreme Court over the past two decades. 

The National Association for Gun Rights, National Rifle Association (NRA), other gun rights groups and 26 Republican state attorneys general filed briefs urging the court to take up one or both of the cases. 

“Nothing in the plain text of the Second Amendment mentions the size of a magazine or the specific features of a firearm,” the NRA wrote in the Washington case. “The plain text provides categorical, presumptive protection for all bearable arms.”

Fourteen states have passed restrictions on magazine capacity with limits ranging from 10 and 20 rounds, according to the U.S. Concealed Carry Association, a gun owner group.

In fending off legal challenges, the states have insisted their restrictions comply with the conservative majority’s recent decisions, saying high-capacity magazines aren’t protected by the Second Amendment because they aren’t “arms” and aren’t commonly used for self-defense. 

“Washington’s law is consistent with an unbroken tradition of weapons restrictions stretching back to our nation’s pre-history,” the state wrote to the Supreme Court.

data.title: ObamaCare impasse sets stage for January health care battle
data.description:
Only a holiday miracle can prevent ObamaCare subsidies from expiring on Jan. 1, setting up a major health care fight next month when lawmakers turn their attention to a 2026 spending bill and another potential shutdown fight.  

After dueling partisan bills failed in the Senate on Thursday, attention now turns to the House, where Republican leaders are teeing up a vote on yet another partisan health care package that does not include the enhanced Affordable Care Act (ACA) subsidies. 

Rank-and-file moderates in both parties are agitating for compromise, launching a pair of Hail Mary discharge petitions this week designed to force votes over the opposition of Speaker Mike Johnson (R-La.) and other GOP leaders.  

But the politics of ObamaCare have barely changed since Democrats shut down the government for more than 40 days to try to force a subsidy extension. And lawmakers in both parties say there’s almost no chance that a breakthrough deal will emerge in the six legislative days that remain before Congress leaves Washington for the long December break.  

“I would not bet on that at all. I think it’s destined for January,” said Rep. Jeff Van Drew (N.J.), a centrist Republican who’s clamoring for Congress to extend the subsidies. “Sometimes things that are extraordinary happen. But that would be extraordinary. In this place, much less six days, we can’t get things done sometimes — either party — in six months.” 

The next government funding deadline is Jan. 30, and Democrats hope to continue to elevate health care costs to mobilize voters in the 2026 midterm elections. 

Some lawmakers said they hope that the Senate’s failure can spark the bipartisan agreement that’s been absent for months. 

“Today was a bit of a messaging exercise,” Sen. Lisa Murkowski (R-Alaska) told reporters Thursday. “Sometimes around the Senate, we have to demonstrate what we can’t do first before we can get to what we need to do. Today was the first step in that process of demonstrating what we can’t do. Now, let’s get on with it.” 

Murkowski was one of four GOP senators to vote for the Democratic bill, which would have extended the enhanced subsidies for another three years. She also voted for the GOP alternative, which would have let the subsidies expire and used that money to fund personal health savings accounts (HSA). 

“Americans are paying the price for this body not working together in the way that it should. And so I’m hopeful that we’ll have some fruitful conversations moving forward,” said Sen. Katie Britt (R-Ala.).  

But others say the damage is already done. 

“Once Jan. 1 comes and everyone is locked into their insurance proposals, you can’t put the toothpaste back in the tube,” Senate Minority Leader Chuck Schumer (D-N.Y.) said after the Democratic bill failed. “Look, the Republicans voted against this 13 times. The onus is on them.” 

If the enhanced subsidies expire at the end of the month, premium costs for tens of millions of Americans enrolled in ACA plans will spike. Neither party wants to be blamed by voters if that happens, and lawmakers are still pointing fingers at each other.  

Senate Majority Leader John Thune (R-S.D.) told reporters that the next steps are going to depend on the willingness of Democrats to compromise. 

“The question is, do the Democrats, after they got their political messaging vote done, actually want to engage in a real conversation about this? Because it didn’t seem like they had a real high level of interest in reforms,” Thune said. 

There are at least two proposals in the Senate that would pair a short-term subsidy extension with reforms such as income caps and mandatory minimum premiums, but Thune declined to put either of them up for a vote against the Democratic bill.  

In the House, Johnson is vowing to vote on a health care package next week. That GOP proposal would not extend the ObamaCare subsidies, however, and is not expected to pass in the Senate, if it even makes it that far.  

The failure of the House bill to address the expiring subsidies sparked a revolt from moderate Republicans on Wednesday, leading a group of centrists to launch a pair of discharge petitions designed to sidestep Johnson’s opposition and force the bills to the floor.  

One of those bills, sponsored by Reps. Brian Fitzpatrick (R-Pa.) and Jared Golden (D-Maine), would extend the tax credits for two years and features a series of reforms to the subsidies including new income eligibility limits, the elimination of no-premium plans and the promotion of HSAs.  

The other, sponsored by Reps. Jen Kiggans (R-Va.) and Josh Gottheimer (D-N.J.), features a one-year subsidy extension, with less stringent changes to eligibility.  

The issue has put GOP leaders in a no-win position: Either they let out-of-pocket health costs skyrocket for millions of voters ahead of next year’s midterms, or they support an Obama-era law they’ve fought for 15 years to repeal.  

But the issue also poses a dilemma for Democratic leaders. Their three-year “clean” subsidy extension bill failed, and they now face pressure to endorse one of the two bipartisan bills or risk being blamed for the impasse. 

“It’s in the Democrats’ [court], because I think it behooves them to support the bipartisan discharge petitions that are there,” Van Drew said. 

Both discharge petitions already have the endorsement of more than 10 Republicans — an extraordinary demonstration of unrest among the GOP rank and file. But if either petition is to succeed, it will require an overwhelming majority of Democrats to sign on. And many of them said they’re waiting for marching orders from House Minority Leader Hakeem Jeffries (D-N.Y.).  

“I think we’ve got to do one or the other,” said Rep. Gregory Meeks (D-N.Y.). “We need to get something over to the Senate and see what they do. So it’s got to be one of those. We’ve got to discharge something. And I’ve just got to determine which one is the best.” 

Jeffries on Thursday declined to weigh in on the competing proposals.  

Democrats say they favor the Kiggans-Gottheimer proposal, because it doesn’t restrict eligibility as much as the Fitzpatrick-Golden bill. But some said they’re also holding out for any movement from Republican leaders, including President Trump, whose administration floated a two-year extension earlier in the month. 

In the Senate, Murkowski said she was “encouraged” by the bipartisan discharge petitions. 

“That’s kind of a big step. That’s not waiting for the president to give an endorsement. That’s not waiting for Speaker Johnson to say this is what we’re going to do,” Murkowski said. 

“At some point in time, you’ve got to start saying what you’re for, right? And so that’s the momentum that I think we’re seeing over in the House. … You’re seeing people put their name behind a proposal, and that, I think, shows momentum,” she added.

data.title: Trump promises White House ballroom completion in ‘about a year and a half’
data.description:
President Trump on Thursday promised the new White House ballroom will be complete in close to a year and a half — seemingly before the end of his second term if his pledge proves true.

“I want to welcome you all to the White House. It’s an honor to have you. It’s a special place. You know, for 150 years, they’ve been trying to do a ballroom,” the president said during the Congressional Ball Thursday evening.

“They never got it up, but we got it off. In a very short period of time, like about a year and a half, you’re going to have the best ballroom anywhere in the country,” he added.

Trump’s new ballroom has been the subject of much controversy in recent months, with the president’s East Wing demolition to create space for the project receiving heavy backlash.

The ballroom was slammed as a “vanity project” by Senate Minority Leader Chuck Schumer (D-N.Y.). Sen. Richard Blumenthal (D-Conn.) referred to the destruction of the East Wing as “heartbreaking.”

“Oh you’re trying to say the cost of living is skyrocketing? Donald Trump can’t hear you over the sound of bulldozers demolishing a wing of the White House to build a new grand ballroom,” Sen. Elizabeth Warren (D-Mass.) previously said on social media.

Last Saturday, President Trump defended lofty costs linked with the ballroom project, saying to critics that the space is going to be “much bigger and more beautiful” when done.

data.title: Gingrich: ‘Republicans are in real trouble’ if economy doesn’t ‘recover’
data.description:
Former Speaker Newt Gingrich (R-Ga.) said Thursday that the GOP is “in real trouble” if the economy does not “recover.”

“Look, I think it’s pretty straightforward. If the economy recovers, as you and I think it will, Republicans will keep the House and increase their margin in the Senate,” Gingrich told Fox Business Network’s Larry Kudlow on “Kudlow.”

“If the economy does not recover, Republicans are in real trouble, and I think the president’s goal has to be to continue to cut spending, to continue to cut regulations, to allow the One Big, beautiful Bill to go into effect January 1 and to continue, around the world, marketing investment in the U.S. and purchase of American goods,” he added.

Republicans lawmakers’ concerns have been recently piqued over signals next year’s midterm elections might be a landslide against the GOP that could result in them losing control of the House and having their Senate majority shrink.

Senate Republicans previously said the off-year elections in New Jersey, Virginia and other American regions in early November were a wake-up call and advised that President Trump and GOP congressional leaders had to confront worries over the slowing economy and high costs.

But Speaker Mike Johnson (R-La.) said Thursday he is “very, very bullish about the midterms.”

“I know that we are going to win because we have a great record to run on,” the Louisiana Republican told The Hill. “We have better candidates, better message and we have a lot to show the people, so we’ll continue our momentum.”

The House Democrats’ campaign arm recently put five more races on its offensive battleground target list for next year, with recent election victories making the party feel more confident about the 2026 midterms.

data.title: Meet the candidates running to be the next U.S. Senator from Texas: James Talarico
data.description:
AUSTIN (Nexstar) — The U.S. Senate race in Texas is quickly shaping up to be one of the most contentious elections across the United States in the 2026 cycle. Since 2003, the seat has belonged to Sen. John Cornyn, R-Texas, who is aiming for a fifth term.

State Rep. James Talarico, D-Round Rock, started his political career in 2018 when he flipped a Texas House seat from Republican to Democratic in a special election to replace retiring State Rep. Larry Gonzales. 

Talarico often cites his experience as a teacher at Rhodes Middle School in San Antonio as his reason for running. According to Census Reporter, 42.7% of people who reside in Rhodes’ zip code live below the poverty line.

Nexstar State Capitol Correspondent Dylan McKim sat down for a 1-on-1 interview with Talarico on Sunday. This is the third in Nexstar’s series of profiles of the candidates running to be the next U.S. Senator from Texas. The following transcript is only edited for clarity and readability.

Sit-down with State. Rep. James Talarico

Dylan McKim: I want to really dig into who James Talarico is. You were a former teacher here in San Antonio. You served in a Texas house and you are now in seminary school.

That’s what we can read in the articles when you look at James Talarico. But for people who want to dig deeper besides those articles, I’ll give you a chance here to explain to our viewers across this entire state: who is James Talarico?

James Talarico: Well, I’m an eigth-generation Texan. My family’s been here since it was Mexico.
My entire extended family lives in the Round Rock area. My parents, my younger sister, brother-in-law, my aunts and uncles, and cousins and grandparents, and I just became an uncle two-and-a-half years ago for the first time, which is definitely the best title I’ve gotten so far.
And so when I’m not in the legislature, when I’m not at the seminary, when I’m not preaching or campaigning, I’m usually with them — either my immediate family or my extended family.

DM: I want to talk about the seminary. What is that like? What are you doing actively in seminary? What part of the process are you in your studies?

JT: So I am kind of in the middle of my seminary journey. I graduated in May with a Master’s of Theological Studies. But I’m hoping to go back to finish my Masters of Divinity, which is what I need to become an ordained minister in the Presbyterian Church.

DM: Is that your main goal?

JT: Yes. It’s kind of like passing the bar after you go to law school. So, I want to finish seminary and then I want to get ordained so that I can be officially a minister in the Church.

DM: And why is that a big deal for you? Why do you want to be a minister in church?

JT: Well, you know, my granddad was a Baptist preacher. And when I was young, he told me that we follow the two commandments that Jesus gave us: to love God and to love neighbor.

In my public service as a public school teacher here in San Antonio, but also as a public official in the state legislature, that’s how I love my neighbor. Through public policy: bringing down the cost of childcare, the cost of housing, the cost of prescription drugs, I’m trying to make my neighbor’s life a little easier.

But to sustain that difficult work, it has to be rooted in a love of God. And going to church every Sunday is how I do that. But being in the legislature is a tough job, and I realized I needed more of a sturdy spiritual foundation to do that work, and that’s when I made the decision to go to seminary and take my faith to the next level. And it’s been one of the best decisions I’ve ever made.

DM: Has it changed you? Has it changed the way you think about politics or the way you approach a problem or the way you talk to people?

JT: Yes, absolutely. After doing this political work for eight years in the legislature, I’ve come to see how the roots of so many of our problems, political problems, economic problems, social problems, those roots are spiritual. There is a spiritual crisis in this country.

The ties that bind us together are starting to unravel. And that’s going to require a spiritual solution. That’s going to come from our faith leaders, our houses of worship, but it’s also got to come from ordinary Texans rediscovering their connections to their neighbors and learning again how to love everyone in our communities. And I’m trying to do that in my capacity, not just as a public official, but hopefully as a minister.

DM: Before you got here today, one of your staff members was in the middle stage here and was kind of pumping the crowd up, and she said, you know, ‘I want you to turn to your neighbor, someone you didn’t come here with, and say, ‘Hi, why do you support James, get to know each other.’’ And I heard literally somebody next to me say, ‘Oh, it’s like we’re in church.’ Is that the environment you want at these rallies, [do] you want people to feel like they’re at church?

JT: Not quite. Today’s a Sunday. We were at church over this morning at Travis Park United Methodist Church in downtown San Antonio where we were feeding some of our homeless neighbors and then we gathered with those congregants for worship.

But I do think that our politics needs to have a stronger spiritual foundation and that means rediscovering our connections with one another, and learning how to love each other despite our differences and despite our disagreements. That’s the only way a democracy can survive. It’s the only way this American experiment can continue, if we remember that there’s far more that unites us than divides us.

Part of that is building community. And so it’s why we bring people together in real life. The algorithms on our social media platforms divide us and pit us against each other on an hourly basis. And I think gathering together in person in the flesh and blood, and meeting our neighbors can be the antidote to that kind of division that we see online.

DM: How do you connect with somebody who maybe isn’t spiritual, doesn’t have a faith, maybe they don’t view that as an importance in their life. How do you connect with that specific voter?

JT: Well, you know, I found in my experience, most people have a spiritual dimension to their lives.

Even if they’re not religious, or they don’t belong to any organized religion, they still have some kind of practice to help ground them, to keep them centered, and keep them connected with other people. And that’s universal across the world. It’s a fundamental part of the human experience. And I think it needs to be more present in our civic discourse and in our political relationships with each other.

DM: ‘Love thy neighbor,’ that’s a big part of your campaign. You’re doing events where you’re trying to show you’re helping the community out. You’ve been on the campaign trail for a little bit now. I remember back in August, there was a Texas Politics Project poll that came out about James Talarico, and a big number in that poll was 54% didn’t know who you were.

What are you doing to get your name out there? You had the Joe Rogan interview, you had the momentum from the quorum break. I think those were big hits for you, for your name and for your identity. But have you been able to continue with that momentum and grow your name and your brand across the state?

JT: Well, that’s the purpose of the campaign. It’s to go out to every corner of the state and earn people’s trust, earn their respect, and hopefully earn their support. And just in the first three months, we have traveled to every corner of Texas, from Beaumont to El Paso, from Amarillo to Brownsville and everywhere in between.

We’ve been to big cities and small towns. We’ve been to red areas and blue areas alike. And we’re meeting people one-on-one. We’re hearing about their hopes and their dreams and their fears.

My goal is for the campaign to respond to those needs that people have. And so it’s going to be a process. We’re kind of in the middle of this primary campaign. We’ve got a long way to go to the primary. We’ve got a long way to go until the general election. But I’m going to keep working at this.

I’m probably the least well-known candidate in any of these races. I’m the new guy on the block. And I’ve got my work cut out for me to meet more people and build more relationships all over Texas.

DM: I think you and your other Democratic candidates, you have your work cut out to try to get some of these Republican voters in Texas or independent voters to say, ‘we want a Democrat in a statewide position for the first time in three decades.’

Have you been able to meet with registered Republicans? Have you been able to meet with people who voted for Trump in [20]16, [20]20 and [20]24? And have you been able to persuade them? I mean, what are those conversations like? You’re shaking your head like you did.

JT: Well, in fact, many of the people that came here today to our event in San Antonio told me that they are not Democrats. They’re either independents or they’re Republicans. And they were curious to hear my vision and my values. And they’re open to an alternative because
I think I speak for a lot of people in the state on both sides of the aisle that we are fed up with one-party rule.

Whenever you’ve got one party, whether it’s the Republican Party or the Democratic Party in charge at every level, it always leads to extremism and corruption because there’s no competition, there’s no accountability, there’s no check on that power. We’ve had one party rule in this state for 30 years, and we’ve seen the extremism and the corruption that’s produced right here in Texas.

So, I’m trying to campaign, not just as a Democrat, but as a Texan, who wants to see a government that responds to the needs of the people, not just billionaire megadonors.

DM: You said you and your Democratic state lawmakers were scrappy. More scrappy than your national Democratic counterparts.

What are you most proud [of]? It could be policy, it could be debates you had on the House floor. In your time in the State House, what are you most proud of?

JT: That’s a hard question because I’ve been able to accomplish a lot in the state legislature as a Democrat in a Republican-dominated body. I’ve been able to work across the aisle to pass the first ever cap on insulin copays in Texas history. I passed a bill to import cheaper prescription drugs from Canada right here into Texas to save lives across our state. I passed one of the biggest early childhood bills in recent memory and I passed a bill that’s going to bring down housing costs for people all over Texas. So there’s a lot to be proud of in my work as a state legislator.

I want to take those fights — those fights to bring down costs, those fights against billionaire mega donors — I want to take those fights to our U.S. Senate so that we can finally have a federal government that works for us and not just the ultra wealthy.

DM: Yeah, I want to talk about that. If you do get elected to represent Texas in the U.S. Senate, what’s the day one issue you’re going after? Like you hit the ground running up there in D.C., what do you want to accomplish?

JT: I’ve said that the first bill I would file is a national ban to end gerrymandering once and for all. Because I think this corruption at the heart of our political system is what’s preventing us from making progress on health care, on education, on housing, on bringing down costs at the grocery store. And so we’ve got to tackle that corruption head-on.

Taking big money out of our politics, banning super PACs, banning billionaires from making unlimited secret donations, banning members of Congress from trading stocks and enriching themselves and banning gerrymandering so we can have free and fair elections all across Texas and all across the country. That’s going to be my top priority because I think that’s the biggest problem we face in this country.

DM: I think you even told me this once, back when we were covering the state [legislature], you said, or maybe it’s just a general statement that Republicans and Democrats at the state level, you guys still seem to get along. You still have lunches and dinners with each other, your families know each other, you’re friends, you still have some sort of working relationship. At the national level, it appears we have hit an era where Democrats are with the Democrats, and Republicans are with the Republicans, and there’s a very clear divide.

So you’re coming from a one-party rule state, and you were to be elected, you’d be a Democrat in the Senate. How would you try to bridge the gap up there? How would you try to get Republicans to work with you?

JT: Well, I don’t think you should ever believe something a politician says, I think you should look at what a politician has done. And I’ve served for eight years in the state legislature in Texas. I have a public voting record. I have a public record on what bills I’ve filed and what bills I’ve passed. I have a public record on the debates I’ve had on the House floor and the questions I’ve asked in the committee.

So I encourage all Texans to take a very close look at that record because what they’re going to find is that I’ve been able to stand up to my Republican colleagues when I believe that they’re hurting my constituents, are hurting people all over the state of Texas. When they’re trying to defund public education, when they’re trying to rob people of their health care. But then I’m also able to work across the aisle when we do agree, and pass major legislation to bring down the cost of housing and childcare and prescription drugs.

So we need a legislator who can do both of those things and good leaders can do both. You can fight back and you can get stuff done. That’s what I’ve already been able to do as a legislator and it’s what I want to do as a U.S. Senator.

DM: You mentioned on the stage here that you wanted to eliminate trillionaires — you want to tax the rich. You want to use that money to pay for health care, housing and education. Could you give us more of a plan that you have in place? Do you have a plan for that?

JT: Yeah, we have to have a tax code where billionaires — and I guess trillionaires, because that’s now the world we live in — are paying their fair share so that all of us can succeed and prosper and grow together.

That’s what we had in the 20th century and when we had an economy where we have the largest middle class in American history. That middle class is disappearing as we speak. The top 1% in this country now owns more wealth than the entire middle class.

Three guys: Elon Musk, Mark Zuckerberg, and Jeff Bezos, own more wealth than 165 million Americans combined. And that’s an unacceptable amount of inequality. And so my goal is to restructure this economy so that we can support working-class Texans and middle-class Texans more than we support billionaires and trillionaires.

I think most Texans want to see that kind of economy where people can actually live the American dream. I read a stat that 90% of baby boomers went on to earn more money than their parents. For millennials like me, it’s 50%. For Gen Z, it’s even lower. So the American dream is literally slipping out of reach for most young people in this country. I want to fight for that dream, revitalize it so it can be a reality for more Americans and more Texans.

DM: What’s your stance on immigration?

JT: I think Texans understand immigration better than most people around the country, because we live with the consequences. We know the challenges and we know the benefits of it.

I’ve said that I think our southern border should be like our front porch. There should be a giant welcome mat out front and a lock on the door. Those aren’t mutually exclusive goals.

We can welcome immigrants who want to come here and contribute and live the American dream and make our communities stronger and richer. And we can also have a system that keeps out people who mean to do us harm.

People have a right to know who’s coming into our country. And the failures on our southern border have opened the door to this extremism that we’re currently seeing — masked men in unmarked vehicles kidnapping people off the street, tearing parents from their children. They’re waiting in school pickup lines. They’re waiting in hospital waiting rooms. And so we should be cracking down on the cartels, not our communities. We should be deporting gang members, not moms and babies.

I think that is what most Texans and most Americans want to see — they want to see a policy that’s pro-immigrant and pro-security. We don’t have that right now. Both political parties have failed us on this issue. I plan on fighting for that kind of middle ground if I’m elected as U.S. Senator.

DM: What does a middle ground look like? Does that [mean] continue building the border wall? We’re in the middle of Operation Lone Star, and the governor told me a couple weeks ago that’s not going to stop anytime soon, at least not during the Trump era. So what does that look like security-wise?

JT: I think it looks like comprehensive immigration reform, which is more border security, more border patrol agents reforming our ports of entry so that we can modernize these entry points and detect the fentanyl that’s coming into this country. 93% of the fentanyl that gets into America comes through the ports of entry, but we don’t have the resources or the manpower or the technology to stop it. That’s something that we could do at the federal level.

I actually worked with Republicans in the state house to respond to the crisis we’re seeing in Texas of fentanyl overdoses. I passed a bill to equip every school in Texas with Narcan, which is a medication that can reverse fentanyl overdoses. This is an issue I’ve cared a lot about.

We also need to reform our asylum system. We have to relieve the visa backlog. We’ve got to hire way more immigration judges so we can process these cases, so there’s not this kind of build up in our system. All of those things I think will make us stronger and safer at the end of the day.

DM: I want to talk about this primary we’re in. We know you’re going up against Colin Allred. You may be going up against Jasmine Crockett, that’s to be determined. But how do you differentiate yourself from either Allred, or for multiple candidates in this democratic race to be this standalone candidate?

[Editor’s note: this interview took place the day before Allred stepped out of the race and Crockett officially announced her candidacy for Senate.]

JT: Well, I’m not interested in tearing down my fellow Democrats. I am out here trying to make my case for why I think I’m the best nominee going into the November election.

I first got elected to the state house by flipping a Trump district that no one thought was winnable. I did that by building a big tent of Democrats, independents, and some Republicans. I want to do that at the state level. I also have a track record of getting stuff done in the state legislature. Fighting back against Republican extremism, but also passing major bills to bring down costs for working Texans.

I think that kind of track record, both electorally and legislatively, is exactly what people want to see from a Democratic nominee, and ultimately what Texans want to see from the U.S. Senator.

DM: You talked a lot about your upbringing and your mom today. What does she think of you running for the U.S. Senate?

JT: You know, I think she’s proud of the campaign that we’re running. The reason I ran for the legislature was to help working people like my mom, like my students, and I’ve been able to do that as a state representative. I think I can do that as a US senator. And my mom was here today at the event. She’s been able to join us at some of the stops on the campaign trail. And sometimes she worries about me working too hard, but I think she’s really proud of the campaign we put together.

DM: Is she sharing any ideas with you?

JT: Always. Mom’s always got ideas.

DM: Any ideas you’re implementing?

JT: I think what we’ve tried to do in our policy platform around paid family leave comes directly from her experience as a working mom who didn’t have the ability to take time off from work to spend with me or my sister. That policy proposal that I’m pushing for comes directly from her experience.

DM: What can you tell Democratic voters and independent voters that you’re the guy to break the streak and you’re the guy that’s going to be the first Democrat in 30 years to win a statewide position?

JT: Well again, you shouldn’t listen to what I say. You should look at what I’ve done. I was able to flip the district that Democrats hadn’t won in 30 years. And that’s exactly the kind of track record I bring to this race.

We’re here in San Antonio. The reason I got into politics is because of my students that I’ve taught here on the west side of the city. I entered the classroom in fall of 2011, right after the state legislature cut $5 billion from our schools. I saw students look at the cracks. I saw students fall behind because of those cuts.

In one class, I had 45 kids in one classroom. And there weren’t enough desks for all those kids, so I had students sitting on the air conditioning unit. In the richest country in the world, in the 21st century, children are sitting on the air conditioning unit. And that got me mad.

And it’s why I stepped up as a 28-year-old and ran for office for the first time, and it’s why I’m running for this U.S. Senate seat. Because my students, students just like them, working people all over the state need a champion in the U.S. Senate, and I want to be that champion.

DM: Anything else you want to add, sir?

JT: I don’t think so.

DM: Are you being noticed more? Can you go out?

JT: Yeah, yes. Yeah, I guess it’s just a byproduct of running around the state and having your name all over the place.

DM: Well, I guess it’s a good sign that your name identity is rising..

JT: I guess so.

DM: Well, that’s all I have for you. Thank you so much. I appreciate your time.
